title: 第 27 節
en: The Adversaries of Herakles.

His enemies are Here, Ares, Poseidon, and their offspring ; his friends are Athene and Apollon. Typhon, Echidna, Orthros, Kerberos, Eurytion, Menoitios, Nephele, Nyx and Erebos, and even 624 THE ADVERSARIES OF HERAKLES. [chap.

Eurystheus, all show traces of a nocturnal or an infernal origin. But the modifications of the original mythological elements brought together to form the labours of Herakles have been very considerable. We saw that the lion of Nemea may have owed its origin to a misunderstanding, and the same has been maintained even by ancient authorities with regard to the apples of the Hesperides. The Greek fxfjXa meaning both apples and herds, it was supposed that, as in the case of the herds of Geryones and of Augeias, Herakles * had to rescue the herds of the West also, herds which were originally the same as the cows so constantly mentioned in the Veda, viz. either the days carried off by the night, or the rainclouds held captive by VWtra, the Panis, and other powers of darkness, to be rescued by Tndra and other well-known gods of light. This would be a case of what has been called a disease of language, but in the narrowest sense of the word, and not in the sense in which I ventured to call the whole of mythology an affection or pathos of language and thought.

The Golden Apple. There is, however, another explanation of the golden apples which has been suggested by Mannhardt in his Lettische Sonnenmythen, where he shows that the sun, after setting, was spoken of as a golden apple that had fallen from a tree. To bring back this apple would originally have meant to bring back the sun from the West where it had vanished, to the East where it was to rise agfain, a work which could be performed by a solar hero only, that is, by a half-human representative of the sun. (Mann- 1 Herakles himself is called Melon and Melios.

Vl] THE GOLDEN APPLE. 625 hardt, Lettische Sonnenmythen, pp. 103, 234.) We must not attempt to be too positive in our reading of the riddles of mythology. The general character, however, of the labours of Herakles, as just explained, seems to me beyond the reach of scientific doubt, while with regard to smaller details we must never forget that the license of the ancient story-tellers was as great as that of the authors of our modern historical novels.

The Hind of Keryneia. We can well understand why the killing of lions, of giants and robbers, should have been ascribed to a son of Zeus, even though it had been performed by a local hero, but the killing of a hind hardly seems to require the intervention of a Herakles. Yet one of his great achievements, the fifth, is said to have been the catching of a hind. It is true that it was a peculiar hind, just as the eland was, the catching of which proved the most difficult task for Lemminkainen, before he could win the maid of Pohjola1. It was a hind with golden horns and brazen feet, and scientific people, beginning with Aristotle (Poetics, xxv, 5), have been at great pains to point out the ignorance of Pindar (01. iii, 31), in speaking of a hind with horns. They ought to have remembered, however, that though real hinds may have no horns, least of all, golden horns, a myth is free to speak of golden horns, and has as much right to that poetical license as it has to the brazen feet which are ascribed to the same hind. Even in our time learned zoologists have actually wasted their time in trying to prove that there is one species of 1 Kalewala, rune B, v. 29.
626 THE HIND OF KERYNEIA. [chap.

the cervine race in which the female has antlers, viz. the reindeer of the North of Europe and Asia. Now as Herakles is supposed to have gone to the North in his chase of the Keryneian hind, the fact of these antlered reindeer hinds has been supposed to prove the reality of the exploit ascribed to Herakles. Others have tried to show that we need not suppose that Herakles actually went as far as Norway or Iceland in search of his hind, because antlers of the reindeer have been discovered in the lake-dwellings in Switzerland, or as Dr. Nestle points out (Academy, Dec. 1st, 1894), near the spring of the Schussen (a little river flowing into the lake of Constance), some twenty kilometres from the nearest point of the Danube or Ister, the shady sources of which Herakles is said to have reached in his journey in search of the golden-horned hind.

All this shows how myths ought not to be treated. Surely we are no longer in that stage of mythological science when people tried to discover the cradle of Jupiter in Krete, or the palace of Priamos in Hissarlik, or the bones of Arthur in the island of Avalon. Was Herakles really no more than a mighty hunter before the Lord, a Grant or Speke who went out to shoot lions and steenboks, more particularly steenboks with brazen feet and golden horns ! The essential points in the hind-myth are that the hind is never tired, that Herakles has to follow her for a whole year, that the animal, when tired at last, returns to Arkadia on the same road on which it had fled, and takes refuge in a sanctuary of Artemis. Herakles, however, catches it on the bank of the river Ladon, and is on the point of killing it when Apollon and his sister intercede for it, and save its VI J THE HIND OF KERYNEIA. 627 life. No one accustomed to the true ring of mythology can suppose that this hind was a real hind, though it may be difficult to determine with certainty what object it represents. Preller (Griech. Myth, ii, 196) took it for the moon chased by the sun and caught at last by its golden horns, the cornua lunae. But the hind is also a name of the dawn in the Veda. In the Rv. X, 3, 2, we read : Krislmam yat eni 1 abhi varpasa bhiit, ' When the bright (hind) overcame the dark one (night) with her apparition.' It is true that eni, as the feminine of eta, is expressive of bright colour only, but it soon became the regular name for hind, and this is the very process by which mythology arises. People say one thing, but they are understood as meaning another. If then the dawn was called eni, and if that word meant at the same time hind, what was more natural than that a hero, representing the sun, should have been conceived as chasing or following the dawn (eni), and as catching her at last, though not destroying her altogether, but letting her live for another day at the instance of Apollon and Artemis? The stories of the moon and the dawn often run together, and we need not attempt to separate what probably, in the earliest periods of mythological speech, was not always sharply separated 2.
Kerberos. One of the most famous monsters overcome by Herakles was Kerberos, of the same kith and kin as Typhaon, Echidna, Orthros, Eurytion, Geryones, 1 Read eni instead of enim.
2 In the Lettish songs we meet with a wether with golden horns (p. 243), meant for the sun.
628 KERBEROS. [chap.

Menoitios, &c. They are all powers of darkness, nocturnal and infernal powers that have to be fought again and again by the representatives of light. That Kerberos was a representative of the night, and that his name is connected with the name of nig-ht, .yarvari, was one of my earliest identifications of Greek and Sanskrit mythologies. It was at once adopted by Weber, Benfey, Aufrecht and others. I had not said that Kerberos was sabala, though sabala was connected with sarvara. The loss of r is startling, but I pointed out the analogous cases of savara and sarvarika, both meaning barbarian, of sarvara, dark, mischievous, and savara, low, vile. I also pointed to passages where it is said of the Dawn that by her light she makes all things that had been coloured by nocturnal darkness (tamasa sarvarena digdhani) to appear as if they had been washed. That sarvari was understood in India as a name of the night is shown by such names of the moon as /Sarvaripati or £arvarisa, which continued to be used in later Sanskrit. Much new evidence has since accumulated to confirm my conjecture, as I pointed out in a paper on ' Prof. Bloomfield's Contributions to the Interpretation of the Veda,' in the Academy, Aug. 13, 1892. Thus in the Ka^aka-samhita XVII, 14, it is simply stated that the two dogs of Yama were day and night. And in the Ka^.-brahma?ia we read : ' Cabala, the speckled, is the day, iSyama, the dark, is the night.' Here we must remember what I often pointed out before, that the divine representatives of any physical phenomena represent these phenomena in a very general way. Thus sabala, the speckled day, instead of being always the day in all its solar brightness, may stand for the beginning of the day, or for the Vl] KERBEROS. 629 first peep of the morning, that is the Kerberos or >Sarvara whom Herakles just drags up to the light and then lets slip again into Hades. The same applies to Orthros or VWtra. Sometimes these two dogs represent not only day and night, but even sun and moon. Thus while in Greek mythology the representative of the dim twilight survived in Kerberos, in India the dog who represented the morning and the morning sun assumed far greater prominence, and it is clear from passages collected by Professor Bloomfield in his Contributions that DivyaA sva, i.e. the heavenly dog, became afterwards simply the sun. Thus we read in Ath.-veda VI, 80 : —

' He (the sun) flies through the air, looking down upon all beings, we desire to do homage with havis to thee (who art) the majesty of the heavenly dog,' or, more literally, ' That which is the majesty of the heavenly dog, under that form we worship thee.'

' In the waters is thy origin, in heaven thy abode, in the midst of the sea and upon the earth thy greatness. That which is the majesty of the heavenly dog, under that form we worship thee with this havis.'

But the moon also was called the heavenly dog. In £at.-br. XI, 1, 5, 1, we read : 'He (the moon) is the heavenly dog ; he watches the animals of the sacrifice.' If then sun and moon, day and night, are called the heavenly dogs, the dogs of Yama, the god of death, that thought was evidently suggested by the fact that day and night, or sun and moon, go on for ever looking out for men, and at last hunting them down, like dogs seeking for prey.
If we once knew that the two dogs of Yama were sun and moon, day and night, called also, as we saw, 630 KERBEEOS. [chap.

Cabala (speckled) and 5yama (black), we can understand what is said in the iTMndogya-Upanishad, VIII, 13, where the journey of the soul after death is described, and where we read : ' From the black (syama) I come to the spotted (sabala), from the spotted to the black, that is from the sun to the moon, from the moon to the sun.' Sun and moon, or day and night, in their regular succession are of course the cause of death. Not only is the sun called death, ^Sat.-br. II, 3, 3, 7, and the year called death because it destroys life by means of day and night, but we read in Ka^.-br. II, 9 : ' Day and night are the two arms of death.'

What then is more natural than that in Greek Kerberos should be the dog of Hades, or the dog of death, and if he is called Kerberos = £arvara, can we doubt that Kerberos meant originally speckled and grey, and that Kerberos and #arvara started from the same source.

If a single letter in the equation /Sarvara-Kerberos is wrong, let us have the phonetic rule which it contravenes. But if Dr. Gruppe has his doubts, and if Professor Rhode (Psyche, p. 281) declares that the equation is badly supported, what shall we say ? All that Professor Erwin Rhode says about Kerberos being without a name in Homer and named for the first time by Hesiod, I had fully explained myself, but when the same objections are raised again and again, and the fact that they have been answered is passed over in silence, what are we to do ? Iteration is no argument, but neither is silence. Again, how does the fact that even Greek poets looked upon Kerberos as a kind-hearted and tame dog, prove that others did not look upon him as dangerous. Herakles at all Vl] KERBEROS. 63 1 events did not look upon him as a friendly beast, and in the KepfiepoKiv'&vvos Tdprapoq he was evidently looked upon as dangerous. All these matters require careful study and a certain knowledge, not only of Greek but of Sanskrit" also. Whoever possesses that, will have little doubt that Kerberos was originally one of the many personifications of the dark night, while those who in the Veda and in later Sanskrit read of the two dogs who spy about among men and carry off the victims of death, will easily discover the common background from which iSarvara in the Veda and Kerberos in Greek emerged.

If it be asked what could be the meaning" of the fight of Herakles with Kerberos, and of his dragging him forth from Hades if only for a short time, that is a question difficult to answer in any case. But there is no reason why it should not have arisen from some proverbial saying that the rising sun had grappled with the darkness of the night and let it fall back into the abyss the very moment he himself had risen to the sky victorious.

Many other exploits ascribed to Herakles, beyond his famous twelve labours, confirm, I think, the view which we have taken of him. He is supposed to have joined in the Argonautic expedition, in the Kalydonian chase, nay he is reported to have besieged the old Troy, long before the famous siege described in the Iliad. This alone should suffice to show to what class of sieges the siege of Troy belongs, and who were the warriors engaged in it, in the Argonautic expedition, and in the Kalydonian chase. That the siege of Troy by Agamemnon was not an historical event is surely 632 KERBEROS. [CHAP.

proved, if any proof were required, by the siege being ascribed to other heroes, such as Herakles (II. v, 641), while Helena also was carried off by more than one lover, for instance by Theseus, even before she had become the wife of Menelaos. Whoever believes in Helena as historical, must also believe, as I said before, in her brothers and in the egg of Leda, for in these matters we eannot pick and choose. This, however, by no means excludes the possibility of some historical siege of Troy, which has been lighted up by the rays of another siege beyond the reach of any spade and shovel, but belonging to the heavenly drama that forms the background of so much mythology and so many epic songs in Greece and elsewhere.

In treating of mythological cycles, like that of which Herakles forms the centre, it must never be forgotten that identical names in Greek and Vedic mythology are by no means requisite in order to enable us to see what was the real background of Aryan mythology. We should know it even if the gods and heroes had no names at all. But for all that the name is a great help, and gives to the analyser of myths a ttov crro) which nothing else could supply. In our case the name of Herakles unfortunately does not tell us much, but points only in the most general way to Here, by no means a friend, but rather the declared enemy of Herakles. Still, if Here corresponds to a possible *Svara, a feminine of Svar = sol, she would represent the bright sky or atmosphere conceived as a feminine like Surya, while Herakles might have been meant from the first for the glory or the hero of the bright sky, in some respects even as a human reflection of Vl] KERBEROS. 633 his divine father Zeus. Hence we can understand why the two, Zeus and Herakles, share several epithets in common, such as Alexikakos, Olympios, Idaios, &c. ; they are in fact, in several cases, simply different actors, one divine, the other human, of the same acts. But though this is true with regard to the general character of the god and the hero, we must not expect that we can find a mythological solution of all the exploits assigned to the one or the other. We know from other mythologies that local, historical, and purely fanciful additions were constantly mixed up with ancient and truly mythological lore. It is the unwillingness to recognise these natural limits of mythological interpretation which has caused so many failures in etymological analysis and mythological interpretation by tempting scholars to explain what by its very nature defies interpretation.
The Two with the One.

In looking further for parallels to the old story of the ' Two with the one,' we have to examine next some more of the legends of the Greek Dioskouroi, properly so called, Kastor and Polydeukes. We find their history localised in the Peloponnesos where Tyndareos or -reos was supposed to have ruled. This Tyndareos, like all kings, had of course to be provided with a genealogy. He was represented as the son of Perieres and Gorgophone, and as the brother of Aphareus, Leukippos, Ikarios (father of Penelopeia), and Arene. Perieres was the son of Kynortas (or of Aiolos and Enarete) ; Kynortas, the son of Amyklas ; Amyklas, the son of Lakedaimon and Sparte ; Lakedaimon, the son of Zeus 634 THE TW0 WITH THE ONE. [chap.

and Taygete. Thus the great object was obtained, and the family of the Tyndarides was proved to have descended from Zeus and to rule by the grace of God. These genealogies vary considerably according to different authorities, and they betray their true character in almost every name. The Greeks believed that these royal ancestors had given their names to the localities in which they ruled. It may be so, but more likely the names of these localities, of towns, mountains, and rivers, such as Amyklai, Lakedaimon, Sparte, Eurotas, and Taygete, existed before their time, and were changed, as even now in Scotland, into names of persons, and lastly arranged into real pedigrees. At the head of them we generally have Zeus, or some hero eponymos of a whole race, as, for instance, Lelex, the father of Eurotas, who represents the ancient race of the Leleges in Lakonia, formerly called Lelegia.

After Tyndareos had thus been provided with a divine pedigree, he was represented as the husband of Leda, and as the father of Helena, Klytaimnestra, Kastor and Polydeukes. Here again there are various versions, the oldest being that in the ninth book of the Odyssey, where Leda appears as the wife of Tyndareos, and the mother of Kastor and Polydeukes. The Dioskouroi are there supposed to have died, but to enjoy, for all that, the privilege of being ereprjixepoL, i. e. being dead one day and alive the next. Some traditions represent the Dioskouroi and Helena as children of Zeus and Leda, and as being born together, all three from the same egg, or Helena from one egg, the Dioskouroi from another ; others make Helena and Polydeukes only children of Zeus, Kastor and Klytaimnestra children Vl] THE TWO WITH THE ONE. 635 of Tyndareos. Pindar represents Leda as becoming the mother of Kastor and Polydeukes in the same night, Kastor being the son of Tyndareos, Polydeukes of Zeus. Hence Polydeukes only was immortal, but Kastor mortal till Polydeukes exchanged half of his immortality with his brother. Why Zeus should have been fabled to* have assumed the form of a swan as the father of the Dioskouroi, is more difficult to explain from a purely Greek point of view. But we know that Vedic poets speak of the sun as patanga, the bird, or as arunaA suparnaA (*Sat.-brahm. IX, 2, 3, 18), the red bird, and even as hamsa, goose or swan, and that in other mythologies also the sun is spoken of as a falcon, a vulture, a red flamingo, &c. In Greece we have at least the poetical expression of Aeschylus, who speaks of the sun as Ziqvbs opvis 1. It is quite possible, however, that the swan was really suggested by the egg, and the conception of the sun as an egg is not only natural, but attested in various mythologies, both ancient and modern. The mundane egg is mentioned in the Vedic Brabmanas ; a duck who lays a golden egg in the morning, and a silver egg in the evening (sun and moon), is mentioned in the Russian Marchen collected by Afanasief, while Mannhardt (1. c, p. 226) tells us of a Milanese riddle that is to be solved in the same way : —
Pjov, pjov, La gaijina fa 1' oeuv. ' It rains, it rains, The hen lays an egg/ 1 Cf. Mannhardt, 1. c, p. 233, note. VOL. II. P 636 THE TWO WITH THE ONE. [chap.

that is, the sun will shine when the rain is over. If then we saw that in the Veda the mother of the twins, Sarawyu, was meant for the moment between the retreat of the night and the return of the morning, Leda too, while laying the swan's egg, may have sprung from the same conception. In that case her twins could only be what the twins of Sarawyu were, day and night (Ahani), Kastor and Polydeukes, and her beautiful daughter, the bright dawn. As the twins of Sarawyu, the Asvins were represented as the suitors of Surya, the sun, fern., who carried her off against all the other gods. The carrying off of Phoibe and Hilaeira, the beautiful Leukippides, by the Dioskouroi may therefore be accepted as a very close counterpart of the Vedic legend.
zh: 赫拉克勒斯的對手們。

他的敵人是赫拉（Here）、阿瑞斯（Ares）、波塞冬（Poseidon）及其後代；他的盟友是雅典娜（Athene）和阿波羅（Apollon）。堤豐（Typhon）、厄喀德那（Echidna）、俄爾特洛斯（Orthros）、刻耳柏洛斯（Kerberos）、歐律提翁（Eurytion）、墨諾提俄斯（Menoitios）、涅斐勒（Nephele）、夜神（Nyx）和厄瑞玻斯（Erebos），甚至624 赫拉克勒斯的對手們（THE ADVERSARIES OF HERAKLES）。[章

歐律斯透斯（Eurystheus）的故事，均顯示出夜間或冥界的起源痕跡。然而，匯聚而成赫拉克勒斯十二功業（the labours of Herakles）的原始神話元素經歷了極大的變異。我們已看到涅邁亞獅子或可源於一場誤解，同樣的說法甚至由古代權威學者針對赫斯珀里得斯的金蘋果提出。由於希臘文 μήλα（mêla）兼具蘋果與牲畜兩義，於是有人推測，如同格律翁與奧革亞斯的牲畜一般，赫拉克勒斯應當拯救西方的牲畜，而這些牲畜最初與吠陀中屢屢提及的牛群相同，亦即被夜晚掠奪的白晝，或為弗栗多（Vrtra）、帕尼斯（Panis）及其他暗黑勢力囚禁的雨雲，有待因陀羅（Indra）及其他著名光明之神予以拯救。這將屬於所謂的「語言的疾病」之列，但僅限於最狹隘的意義，而非我曾冒昧稱整個神話學為語言與思想之情動（pathos）或病變的意義。
不過，對於金蘋果還有另一種解釋，曾由曼哈特在其《萊蒂什太陽神話》(Lettische Sonnenmythen) 中提出。他指出，太陽落下之後，人們稱之為從樹上掉下來的金蘋果。最初，要取回這顆蘋果原本意謂著要把太陽從其消失的西方帶回到將要升起的東方，這項工作只有太陽英雄才能完成，即由代表太陽的半人形象來執行。(Mann-¹ 赫拉克勒斯本人被稱為梅隆 (Melon) 和梅利歐斯 (Melios)。
（Hardt, *Lettische Sonnenmythen*, pp. 103, 234.）我們在閱讀神話的謎語時，不應過於武斷。然而，赫拉克勒斯的勞苦任務的整體特徵，如上所述，似乎對我而言已超越科學懷疑的範圍；至於較小的細節，我們務必不忘，古代說故事者的創作自由度與我們現代歷史小說作者的自由度一樣廣泛。

我們完全可以理解，為什麼殺死獅子、巨人和強盜這樣的壯舉會被歸功於宙斯之子，即便這些壯舉實際上是由某位地方英雄所成就的；但殺死一隻牝鹿似乎硬是說不出為什麼需要海克力斯這樣的人物出手。然而，他的偉大成就之一，即第五項任務，據說是捕捉一隻牝鹿。確實，這隻牝鹿很特殊，就如同那隻駝羚一樣，捕捉這隻駝羚被證明是列明凱寧能贏得波約拉少女之前最困難的任務¹。這隻牝鹿長著金角和銅足，自亞里斯多德（《詩學》，二十五，五）以來，科學人士一直費盡力氣指出皮達（《奧林匹克頌歌》三，三一）的無知，因為他提到一隻有角的牝鹿。然而，他們應該還記得，雖然真實的牝鹿可能沒有角，尤其是沒有金角，但神話自由地談論金角，在這方面享有的詩歌特許與談論同一隻牝鹿所具有的銅足一樣充分。即使在我們的時代，博學的動物學家實際上還在浪費時間，試圖證明有某一物種¹ 《卡勒瓦拉》，第八詩章，第二十九行。
626 克瑞涅亞的母鹿。[章節]

鹿科種族，其中雌性長有鹿角，即歐洲和亞洲北部的馴鹿。現在既然赫拉克勒斯據說在追捕克律涅亞牝鹿時前往北方，這些長角的馴鹿牝鹿的事實被認為可以證實賦予赫拉克勒斯的功績的真實性。還有人試圖證明，我們不必假設赫拉克勒斯實際上曾遠赴挪威或冰島去尋找他的牝鹿，因為馴鹿鹿角曾在瑞士的湖居遺址中被發現，或如內斯特爾博士所指出的（《學院期刊》，1894年12月1日），在舒森泉附近（舒森河是流入康斯坦茨湖的小河），距離多瑙河或伊斯特河最近的點約二十公里，據說赫拉克勒斯在尋找金角牝鹿的旅程中曾到達這條河陰暗的源頭。

這一切都說明了神話應該如何正確對待。我們無疑已經不再處於神話學的那個階段，當時人們試圖在克里特島發現朱庇特的搖籃，或在希薩利克發現普裡阿摩斯的宮殿，或在阿瓦隆島發現亞瑟的遺骨。赫拉克勒斯真的只不過是上帝面前的一位威武的獵人，一個葛蘭特或斯皮克那樣的人物，出外獵殺獅子和小羚羊，尤其是那些有著銅足和金角的小羚羊嗎？牝鹿神話中的核心要素在於：這隻牝鹿永不疲倦，赫拉克勒斯必須追蹤她整整一年，當這隻動物最後疲倦時，會循著逃亡時的同一條路返回阿卡迪亞，並在阿耳特彌斯的聖殿中尋求庇護。然而，赫拉克勒斯在拉冬河的河岸追上了它，正要殺死它時，阿波羅和他的妹妹為它求情，拯救了它的生命。任何熟悉神話真正韻味的人都不會認為這隻牝鹿是真實存在的，儘管要確定它究竟代表什麼對象可能困難重重。普雷勒（Preller）在《希臘神話》第二卷第 196 頁中認為它代表被太陽追逐、最終被金角即新月角（cornua lunae）捕獲的月亮。但在吠陀中，牝鹿也是曙光的名稱。在《梨俱吠陀》第十卷第 3 詩第 2 節中，我們讀到：Krishnam yat eni abhi varpasa bhit，「當光明的（牝鹿）用她的顯現克服黑暗的（夜晚）時。」雖然 eni 作為 eta 的陰性形式只表達明亮的色彩，但它很快就成為牝鹿的常規名稱，這正是神話產生的過程。人們說一件事，卻被理解為另一個意思。那麼，如果曙光被稱為 eni，而這個詞同時又意味著牝鹿，那麼代表太陽的英雄被想像為追逐或跟隨曙光（eni），最終捕獲她，但不是完全摧毀她，而是在阿波羅和阿耳特彌斯的請求下讓她活著迎接另一天——還有什麼比這更自然的呢？月亮和曙光的故事常常交織在一起，我們也無需試圖分離在神話語言最早期可能根本沒有被清晰區分的東西。
刻耳柏洛斯。赫拉克勒斯所克服的最著名怪物之一是刻耳柏洛斯（Kerberos），與提豐、厄喀德那、歐耳特洛斯、歐律提翁及革律翁¹同出一源。 ¹參閱 eni 而非 enim。
2 在萊蒂什歌謠中，我們遇見一隻金色角的閹羊（第243頁），其意象代表太陽。
628 刻耳柏洛斯。[章

美諾伊提歐斯（Menoitios）等等。它們都是黑暗的力量，夜間的和冥府的力量，必須一再被光的代表者所對抗。刻耳柏洛斯（Kerberos）是夜晚的代表，而其名字與夜晚的名稱薩爾瓦里（sarvari）相關聯，這是我最早進行的希臘神話與梵文神話的同源識別之一。韋伯（Weber）、本費（Benfey）、奧弗雷希特（Aufrecht）等人立即採納了此說。雖然薩巴拉（sabala）與薩爾瓦拉（sarvara）相關聯，但我並未說刻耳柏洛斯就是薩巴拉。r 音的喪失確實令人驚異，但我指出了類似的情況，如薩瓦拉（savara）和薩爾瓦里卡（sarvarika）都表示野蠻人，薩爾瓦拉表示黑暗、頑劣，而薩瓦拉表示低賤、邪惡。我也指向了一些經文段落，其中據說曉神（Dawn）以其光芒使那些被夜間黑暗所染色的萬物（tamasa sarvarena digdhani）顯現如同已被洗淨。薩爾瓦里在印度被理解為夜晚的名稱，這在月亮的名稱中可得證實，如薩爾瓦里帕蒂（Sarvaripati）或薩爾瓦里沙（Sarvarisa），這些名稱在後代梵文中繼續被使用。自此之後，大量新的證據累積起來以證實我的推測，我在《學院》（Academy）期刊1892年8月13日發表的論文〈布隆菲爾德教授對吠陀的詮釋之貢獻〉中指出了這一點。因此，在《卡塔克梵書》（Ka^aka-samhita）XVII, 14 中，簡單地陳述了亞摩（Yama）的兩隻狗是晝與夜。而在《卡塔梵經》（Ka^.-brahma?ia）中，我們讀到：「薩巴拉，斑駁者，是白晝，斯亞瑪（Syama），昏暗者，是黑夜。」在此我們必須記得我之前經常指出的，任何物理現象的神聖代表者都以非常一般的方式代表這些現象。因此，薩巴拉，斑駁的白晝，與其總是以所有其太陽的光輝形態代表白晝，反不如代表白晝的開始，或晨曦的第一線，即刻耳柏洛斯（Kerberos）或薩爾瓦拉（Sarvara），正是海格力斯（Herakles）剛從中拖出來拉入光中，然後又讓它滑回冥界的那一個。同樣的情況也適用於歐爾特羅斯（Orthros）或弗栗多（VWtra）。有時這兩隻狗不僅代表晝與夜，甚至代表太陽與月亮。因此，儘管在希臘神話中昏暗的暮光的代表者在刻耳柏洛斯中倖存下來，但在印度，代表晨曦和晨陽的狗卻獲得了遠為突出的重要性，而從布隆菲爾德教授在其《貢獻》中蒐集的段落來看，天犬（Divya‧asva），即天界之犬，後來簡單地成為了太陽。因此，我們在《雅柔吠陀》（Ath.-veda）VI, 80 中讀到：
「他（太陽）飛越空中，俯瞰一切眾生，我等願以祭品（havis）向汝致敬——汝是天犬的威嚴。」或者更直譯地說：「天犬的威嚴所在之處，我等以此形相崇拜汝。」
「汝之起源在於水中，汝之居所在於天界，汝之偉大遍及海洋之中及大地之上。天犬之威嚴乃至尊者，吾等以此祭物在該形象下祭祀汝。」
但月亮也被稱為天狗。在《牟坦德羅奧義書》(Śat.-br. XI, 1, 5, 1)中，我們讀到：「他（月亮）是天狗；他監視著祭祀的動物。」如果日與月、晝與夜都被稱為天狗、死神閻摩(Yama)的狗，那麼這一想法顯然是由以下事實啟發的：日與夜，或日與月，永遠不斷地注視著人類，最後像獵犬尋覓獵物一樣將他們追獵下去。
如果我們一旦知曉亞摩（Yama）的兩隻犬是日和月、晝和夜，也被稱為（如我們所見）刻耳柏洛斯（KERBEEOS），[本章

Cabala（斑駁）和 syama（黑色），我們可以理解《慈氏奧義書》第八卷第十三章中所述的靈魂死後的旅程，其中有云：「自黑色（syama）而來至斑駁（sabala），自斑駁而來至黑色，即自太陽至月亮，自月亮至太陽。」太陽與月亮，或日與夜，在其規律的循環交替中當然是死亡的成因。非但太陽被稱為死亡（見《沙達亞特那梵書》第二卷第三篇第三‧七節），年歲也被稱為死亡，因為它藉由日與夜而摧毀生命，我們更在《嘉慈梵書》第二卷第九節中讀到：「日與夜是死亡的兩臂。」
那麼，在希臘語中，刻耳柏洛斯（Kerberos）應該是冥府之犬或死亡之犬，這還有什麼比這更自然的呢？如果他被稱為刻耳柏洛斯（Kerberos）= शर्वर（śarvara），我們豈能懷疑刻耳柏洛斯最初的含義就是有斑點和灰色的，以及刻耳柏洛斯和शर्वर（śarvara）源自同一個根源呢？

如果在方程式 /Sarvara-Kerberos 中有哪怕一個字母是錯誤的，請讓我們看看它違反了哪一條音韻規則。但若格呂佩博士心存疑慮，若羅德教授（《靈魂》第281頁）聲稱這個方程式證據不足，我們又能說什麼呢？關於克爾柏洛斯在荷馬作品中沒有名字、首次由赫西俄德命名這一切，我都已經完整解釋過了；然而當同樣的反對意見一再被提出，而這些反對已被回答的事實卻被無言地掩蓋時，我們該如何是好？重複並不是論證，但沉默也不是。再者，即便希臘詩人們將克爾柏洛斯視為一隻善良而溫馴的狗，這一事實又如何能證明他人不將其視為危險的呢？赫拉克勒斯無論如何都沒有將它視為友善的獸類，而在《克爾柏洛斯下地獄記》中，它顯然被視為危險的。所有這些事項都需要仔細研究，以及不僅對希臘文、而且對梵文的某種程度的認識。凡是具備這些條件者，將不會懷疑克爾柏洛斯原本是黑暗之夜眾多擬人化之一；而那些在《吠陀》和後來梵文文獻中讀到兩隻狗在人間四處偵察並帶走死者受害者相關記載的人，將容易發現共同的源頭，從中 Sarvara 在《吠陀》中和克爾柏洛斯在希臘文中應運而生。
如果有人問赫拉克勒斯與刻耳柏洛斯之戰，以及他將其從哈迪斯拖出來（即使只是短暫時間）的含義是什麼，那麼這個問題在任何情況下都很難回答。但是沒有理由認為它不可能源於某句諺語——太陽升起時與夜晚的黑暗搏鬥，並在其自身勝利地升上天空的瞬間，就將黑暗重新推回深淵。
許多其他歸屬於海拉克勒斯的功業，超越了他著名的十二項勞役，我認為足以確認我們對他所採取的看法。據說他參與了阿爾戈英雄遠航，參與了卡呂冬狩獵，甚至據報他在遠古時代就曾圍攻特洛伊，遠早於《伊利亞德》中所述的著名圍城。此點本身就足以表明特洛伊圍城屬於什麼類別的圍城，以及在阿爾戈英雄遠航和卡呂冬狩獵中參與其事的戰士們是何等人物。阿伽門農圍城特洛伊並非歷史事件，這無疑是 632 刻耳柏洛斯。[第

這一點已經得到證實，如果還需要證據的話，可由圍城一事被歸於其他英雄（如赫拉克勒斯，*II. v, 641*）而足徵；海倫娜亦曾被不止一位情人奪走，例如忒修斯便曾帶走過她，甚至在她尚未成為墨涅勞斯的妻子之前。凡相信海倫娜為歷史人物者，亦必相信——如我前所述——她的兄弟們以及勒達的蛋，蓋在此等事項上，我們無法挑三揀四。然而，這決不排除某一歷史上的特洛伊圍城確實存在的可能性，只不過該事件曾被另一場圍城的光芒所照亮——而此另一場圍城乃超越任何鏟子和鐵鍬所能及，但卻屬於那天上的戲劇，而此天上的戲劇乃是希臘及他處眾多神話與史詩所共同背景的存在。

在論述神話週期（如海拉克勒斯所居中心的那個週期）時，必須切勿忘記，希臘神話與吠陀神話中相同的名字，絕非我們看出雅利安神話真實背景所必需的。即使諸神與英雄完全沒有名字，我們也能知曉它。然而，名字確實大有幫助，它為神話的分析者提供了一種他物所無法供應的基礎（τὸν σκοπόν）。在我們的個案中，海拉克勒斯這個名字不幸並未告訴我們太多，它只是以最寬泛的方式指向赫拉，赫拉絕非海拉克勒斯的友人，反而是他已宣告的敵人。儘管如此，若赫拉對應於可能的 *Svara（陽性 Svar = 太陽的陰性形式），她將代表被構想為陰性的明亮天空或大氣，正如蘇利耶（Surya），而海拉克勒斯自始可能就被意指為明亮天空的榮耀或英雄，在某些方面甚至是其神聖父親宙斯的人類映照。因此我們能夠理解，為何宙斯與海拉克勒斯二者共享若干相同的修飾語，例如阿歷克西卡科斯（Alexikakos）、奧林匹歐斯（Olympios）、伊達歐斯（Idaios）等等；事實上，在許多情況下，他們僅是同一行為的不同演員，一個是神聖的，另一個是人類的。但雖然這對於神與英雄的普遍性格而言是真實的，我們卻不應期待能夠找到對分配給其中任何一者的所有事蹟的神話解釋。我們從其他神話體系知道，地方性的、歷史性的，以及純粹虛構的成分不斷地與古老且真正神話的傳統混雜在一起。正是對神話解釋的自然界限的不願承認，引誘學者去解釋本質上無法解釋之物，因而導致了詞源分析與神話解釋中如此眾多的失敗。
二與一

在尋求「二與其一」之古老故事的進一步並行例證時，我們接著必須審視希臘狄奧斯庫里（Dioskouroi）的更多傳說，確切地說，乃是卡斯托爾（Kastor）與波呂德烏剋斯（Polydeukes）。我們發現他們的歷史被定位於伯羅奔尼撒半島，廷達瑞歐斯（Tyndareos）或蒂恩達瑞歐斯（-reos）據說曾在此統治。這位廷達瑞歐斯，如同所有國王一樣，當然必須被賦予一份族譜。他被表現為佩里厄瑞斯（Perieres）與戈耳貢豐妮（Gorgophone）之子，並為阿法瑞烏斯（Aphareus）、盧基普斯（Leukippos）、伊卡里歐斯（Ikarios，佩涅羅佩亞的父親）和阿瑞涅（Arene）之兄弟。佩里厄瑞斯是基諾塔斯（Kynortas，或為埃俄羅斯與埃那瑞特之子）之子；基諾塔斯是阿彌克剌斯（Amyklas）之子；阿彌克剌斯是拉開第夢（Lakedaimon）與斯巴爾特（Sparte）之子；拉開第夢是宙斯（Zeus）之子。

及泰吉忒。如此一來，偉大的目標便得以實現，廷達瑞德家族被證實乃下降自宙斯，並奉神恩而統治。這些族譜根據不同的權威記載而相差甚鉅，且在幾乎每一個名字中都暴露了其真實的性質。希臘人相信，這些王族祖先曾將他們的名字賦予了他們所統治的地點。或許確實如此，但更有可能的是，這些地點的名字——城市、山脈及河流，如阿姆克萊、拉凱代蒙、斯巴達、歐羅塔和泰吉忒——早於他們的時代就已存在，並如同今日蘇格蘭一樣，被轉化成人名，最後排列成真實的族譜。在這些族譜的首位，我們通常看到宙斯，或是某位代表整個種族的同名英雄，例如勒勒克斯——歐羅塔之父，他代表了拉康尼亞的古老民族勒勒革人，該地曾被稱為勒勒吉亞。

在堤達瑞俄斯（Tyndareos）獲得了神聖的譜系之後，他被描繪成勒妲（Leda）的丈夫，以及海倫娜（Helena）、克呂泰涅斯特拉（Klytaimnestra）、卡斯托爾（Kastor）和波呂底烏刻斯（Polydeukes）的父親。這裡同樣存在著多種版本，其中最古老的版本出現在《奧德賽》第九卷，勒妲在其中被描述為堤達瑞俄斯的妻子，以及卡斯托爾和波呂底烏刻斯的母親。狄奧斯庫里（Dioskouroi）據說已經死亡，但儘管如此仍享受到 ἀμφιθάλεια（amphibaleia），即一天死亡、一天活著的特權。某些傳統將狄奧斯庫里和海倫娜描繪為宙斯和勒妲的孩子，並說他們三人都從同一個蛋中誕生，或海倫娜從一個蛋、狄奧斯庫里從另一個蛋中誕生；另有傳統則只將海倫娜和波呂底烏刻斯視為宙斯的孩子，而卡斯托爾和克呂泰涅斯特拉為堤達瑞俄斯的孩子。品達（Pindar）描述勒妲在同一個夜晚成為卡斯托爾和波呂底烏刻斯的母親，卡斯托爾是堤達瑞俄斯之子，波呂底烏刻斯是宙斯之子。因此只有波呂底烏刻斯是不朽的，而卡斯托爾是凡人，直到波呂底烏刻斯與其兄弟分享了他一半的不朽性。為何宙斯被編造成以天鵝的形態為狄奧斯庫里之父，從純粹的希臘觀點來看更難以解釋。但我們知道吠陀詩人將太陽稱為 patanga（鳥）、或 arunaḥ suparnaḥ（梵文：紅鳥；《沙塔帕塔梵書》IX, 2, 3, 18），甚至稱為 haṃsa（鵝或天鵝）；而在其他神話體系中，太陽也被稱為獵鷹、禿鷲、紅鶴等。在希臘，我們至少有埃斯基羅斯（Aeschylus）的詩化表達，他將太陽稱為 Διὸς ὄρνις（Dios ornis）。然而很有可能天鵝實際上是由蛋所啟發的，而將太陽觀想為蛋不僅是自然的，而且在古今各種神話體系中都有所記載。宇宙蛋在吠陀的《梵書》中被提及；俄羅斯民間故事集編者阿法納西耶夫（Afanasief）所收集的民間故事中提到一隻鴨子，在早晨下金蛋，在晚上下銀蛋（代表太陽和月亮）；而曼哈特（Mannhardt，同上，第 226 頁）告訴我們米蘭方言的一則謎語需要用同樣方式解答：
普約夫，普約夫，拉蓋伊納法奧伊夫。「下雨了，下雨了，母雞生蛋了。」¹ 參見曼哈特，同上，第233頁，註釋。第二卷，636頁。雙與一。

那就是說，雨過天晴，太陽就會照耀。若然我們在吠陀中看到雙胞胎的母親莎羅瓦尤（Sarawyu）象徵夜晚消退與早晨回歸之間的時刻，那麼勒達在產下天鵝蛋時，也可能源於同樣的觀念。在這種情況下，她的雙胞胎只能是莎羅瓦尤的雙胞胎所代表的——晝與夜（Ahani），即卡斯托與波呂德烏克斯；而她那美麗的女兒，則是光明的曙光。如同莎羅瓦尤的雙胞胎，阿斯溫神（Asvins）被表現為蘇利耶（太陽，陰性）（Surya, fern.）的求婚者，並在眾神的反對中將她奪走。因此，迪奧斯庫里神兄弟對婓蓓和希拉埃拉（那美麗的勒烏基帕得斯）的掠奪，可以被視為吠陀傳說的一個極為接近的對應物。
